title: Mansa Musa's Hajj to Mecca
summary: In 1324 Musa I, ninth mansa of the Mali Empire, set out from his capital on the Niger with a caravan of thousands and enough gold to disrupt the currency of Egypt. His pilgrimage to Mecca made him the most famous African ruler of the medieval age. Along the way he built mosques, hired the architect Abu Ishaq al-Sahili, and put West Africa firmly on the maps of the Mediterranean world, from Cairo to Majorca. The trip crystallised Mali's reputation as one of the richest states of the fourteenth century and helped make its city of Timbuktu synonymous with distant wealth.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Mali Empire had been founded in the second quarter of the thirteenth century by Sundiata Keita, who defeated the Sosso king Sumanguru Kante at the battle of Kirina around 1235 and united the Mande-speaking peoples of the upper Niger under a single mansa, or king of kings. Sundiata's successors extended Mali's control over the goldfields of Bure and Bambuk in the southwest and the trans-Saharan trading termini at Timbuktu and Walata in the north, giving the empire a monopoly on much of the gold that flowed by camel caravan across the desert to North Africa, and from there into the Mediterranean and European economies.

Musa came to power around 1312. The Egyptian encyclopaedist al-Umari, writing a decade after the pilgrimage on the basis of interviews with people who had met the mansa, reports Musa's own explanation of his accession: the previous mansa, most likely Muhammad ibn Qu, had sailed off with a fleet into the Atlantic to see what lay on the other side and had never returned, leaving the throne to his deputy. Whatever the precise story, Musa inherited an empire at the height of its territorial reach: some contemporary accounts describe twenty-four vassal kingdoms and a domain stretching from the Atlantic to the loop of the Niger and from the southern edge of the Sahara to the forest belt of West Africa.

Islam had been present in the western Sudan for several centuries, brought by Berber and Arab merchants from the Maghreb. Earlier Mali kings had already accepted the faith and by Musa's reign the court, the merchant class, and much of the urban population of towns like Djenne and Timbuktu were Muslim, though the rural population retained many older beliefs. The hajj, the pilgrimage to Mecca, was one of the five pillars of Islam and a duty for every Muslim with the means. For a sovereign of Musa's wealth it was also a chance to display legitimacy on the largest possible stage and to make the connections with the Mamluk sultanate of Egypt and the Arab scholarly world that a growing Islamic realm needed.

Preparations were on an imperial scale. The chronicler al-Umari and the later Timbuktu historian Mahmud Kati record that Musa mustered a caravan of some 60,000 people, including 12,000 personal attendants dressed in silk and Persian brocade, 500 slaves who marched at the head of the procession each carrying a staff of gold weighing several pounds, and a baggage train of eighty to a hundred camels each loaded with three hundred pounds of gold dust. The figures are inflated in some retellings and clean estimates are impossible, but every serious source agrees that the load of bullion was enormous. Musa also carried personal supplies for himself and his senior wife Inari Konte and her retinue of five hundred female attendants.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The caravan left the Malian capital, which some scholars locate at Niani on the Sankarani river, in the first half of 1324. It moved north across the Sahel to Walata, an old caravan city, and then out into the desert. The Arab historian Ibn Khaldun, writing later on the basis of Malian informants, describes stopping-places at Tuat in what is now southern Algeria before the pilgrimage cut northeast to Cairo. Musa arrived in the Egyptian capital in July 1324 during the reign of the Mamluk sultan al-Nasir Muhammad and camped for three months near the Pyramids at Giza.

An awkward diplomatic incident marked his stay. Custom required visiting rulers to kiss the ground and the sultan's hand in audience. Musa is said to have refused, declaring that he prostrated himself only before God, and to have accepted a compromise only after the intercession of his hosts. Once the protocol was settled, the mansa's largesse became a Cairene sensation. He gave gold to court officials, to Sharifs and jurists, to the Sufi lodges, to the mosque of Ibn Tulun, and to any beggar who approached him. He borrowed further sums from Cairo merchants to finance the rest of the journey, agreeing to repay them at interest on his return.

The effect on the Egyptian economy was measurable. Al-Umari, writing twelve years later, reported that the value of gold in Cairo had not yet recovered and that the mithqal, the standard Islamic gold coin, was still trading at six dirhams instead of ten before Musa's visit. Modern estimates put the drop in gold prices at perhaps 10 to 25 percent in the years following. It remains one of the very few episodes in premodern history in which a single traveller demonstrably shifted a major currency.

From Cairo Musa continued to Medina and Mecca, where he performed the rites of the hajj in the autumn of 1324. Contemporary sources record that he purchased land in the holy cities for the benefit of future pilgrims from the western Sudan and recruited Islamic scholars to accompany him home. Among those who returned with him was the Andalusi poet and architect Abu Ishaq al-Sahili, whom he engaged, according to Ibn Khaldun, for the sum of four hundred mithqals of gold, and who is credited by some traditions with designing an audience hall for the mansa and, more controversially, the Djingereyber Mosque at Timbuktu, though modern historians treat the second claim with caution.

The return journey was slower and less triumphant. Musa reached Timbuktu at the beginning of 1325, formally annexed the city and the neighbouring town of Gao, and by his own accounts sponsored the construction or renovation of mosques, madrasas, and a royal residence there. Somewhere along the return route he learned that his general Sagmandia had captured Gao from the Songhai and brought back two royal hostages, evidence of Mali's continued expansion. By 1326 or 1327 the caravan was back in the capital.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Mansa Musa's pilgrimage had immediate and lasting effects on West Africa. The scholars, jurists, and merchants he brought back settled in Timbuktu, Djenne, and other towns of the middle Niger, deepening the region's connection to the wider Islamic scholarly world and giving Timbuktu the beginning of its later reputation as a centre of learning. The Sankore Mosque, later associated with a famous madrasa, was renovated and expanded in his reign. Trans-Saharan trade in gold, salt, copper, and books grew accordingly; more Malian pilgrims and merchants used the northern routes; more North African scholars and traders travelled south to Mali. The empire's revenues appear to have risen with this expanded commerce.

Musa continued to reign for perhaps another decade, dying around 1332 to 1337. Under his son Maghan I and his brother Sulayman, who took the throne in 1341, Mali remained the dominant power of the western Sudan. Ibn Battuta, the great Moroccan traveller, visited the empire in 1352 to 1353 and left the most detailed description of it we have: a large, orderly, prosperous state, its court elaborately ceremonial, its capital laid out along the Niger, its people conspicuously safe on the roads. He was less impressed by some court practices but scarcely bothered to hide his surprise at how peaceful and well-governed the country was in comparison with much of the fourteenth-century Mediterranean.

The pilgrimage also put Mali on the mental map of Latin Europe. By 1339 the Majorcan cartographer Angelino Dulcert had included a rich West African ruler on his chart of the world. In 1375 Abraham Cresques, another Majorcan Jewish cartographer, drew the great Catalan Atlas, in which Mansa Musa appears seated on a throne in the Sahel, crowned, sceptre in one hand and a huge gold nugget in the other. His likeness is one of the most reproduced medieval images of an African ruler and it is a direct product of the news of his pilgrimage. In the following century Portuguese, Genoese, and Castilian voyagers along the West African coast were driven partly by the search for direct access to the goldfields at whose head Musa had sat.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Mansa Musa has become one of the most famous historical figures of the pre-colonial Africa, in part because a certain kind of retrospective calculation makes him the richest human being in recorded history. Modern estimates of his wealth in adjusted terms are unreliable and depend on many arbitrary assumptions, but the underlying fact is real: at the moment of his reign, the Mali Empire controlled a very large share of the gold flowing into the Mediterranean and Islamic economies, and Musa spent that gold with unusual public visibility.

His pilgrimage is remembered on both sides of the Sahara. Malian and wider West African oral tradition preserves stories about his generosity; Arabic-language chronicles from Cairo and Damascus record his visit; Timbuktu manuscripts collected in modern archives make him a founder-figure of the city's scholarly identity. Modern Malian and pan-African historiography has drawn on him to counter older colonial claims that pre-colonial Africa lacked developed states or literate cultures.

Some elements of the traditional story are now questioned. The chronology of his death is uncertain; the size of his caravan is exaggerated in some sources; the extent of his role in commissioning Timbuktu's landmark mosques is debated. But the essentials, the pilgrimage itself, the flood of gold into Cairo, the currency shock, the recruitment of scholars and architects, the growth of Timbuktu, and the entry of Mali onto European maps, are established beyond serious doubt by an unusually rich body of surviving Arabic sources. Few premodern rulers have left as vivid or as economically consequential a trace on the historical record.

worldContext.text: Europe in the 1320s was still in the shadow of the Great Famine of 1315 to 1317, which had killed millions across the north. In England Edward II was on an unstable throne he would lose to his wife Isabella and her lover Roger Mortimer in 1326. The papacy sat at Avignon under John XXII, embroiled in the conflict with Emperor Louis IV of Bavaria. In Italy Dante Alighieri had died in 1321 leaving his Divine Comedy freshly completed.

China was ruled by the Mongol Yuan dynasty under Yesun Temur Khan, though provincial revolts and Yellow River floods were beginning the long crisis that would culminate in the Ming rebellion of the 1350s. Marco Polo, who had returned from China only in 1295, had died at Venice in January 1324. In Japan the Kamakura shogunate was in its last decade under regent Hojo Takatoki and would fall in 1333.

The Delhi Sultanate under Ghiyath al-Din Tughluq, who died in 1325 in mysterious circumstances involving a collapsing pavilion built by his son, was at the greatest territorial extent it had yet reached, running from Sindh into the Deccan. His son Muhammad bin Tughluq would soon launch his notorious plan to move the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad. In the south the Kakatiya and Hoysala kingdoms were being ground down by Tughluq armies.

In the Valley of Mexico the newly settled Mexica had, according to their own chronicles, founded Tenochtitlan on an island in Lake Texcoco just a few years before, around 1325, and were subordinate to the Tepanec city of Azcapotzalco. In the Andes the Inca dynasty of Cuzco was still a local kingdom; the great expansion under Pachacuti lay a century ahead. Cahokia was in its final decline; ancestral Pueblo people were resettling in the Rio Grande valley.

In Aotearoa Maori society was well established, with the first classic Maori pa fortifications beginning to appear. On Rapa Nui moai construction continued, though environmental pressures were mounting. Polynesian communities across the Pacific pursued long-standing fishing and horticultural economies. In highland New Guinea intensive taro and sweet potato agriculture supported dense populations, and Aboriginal Australians maintained the fire-managed landscapes of the continent.

figures.name: Mansa Musa
figures.role: Ninth mansa of the Mali Empire, reigned c. 1312 to c. 1332 to 1337; his 1324 hajj made Mali famous across the Mediterranean world
figures.credit: Detail from the Catalan Atlas by Abraham Cresques, 1375, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Abu Ishaq al-Sahili
figures.role: Andalusi poet and architect from Granada whom Musa hired at Mecca and brought back to Mali; died at Timbuktu in 1346

figures.name: Al-Nasir Muhammad
figures.role: Mamluk sultan of Egypt who received Musa in Cairo in the summer of 1324

figures.name: Al-Umari
figures.role: Syrian encyclopaedist whose Masalik al-absar preserves the fullest early Arabic account of Musa's pilgrimage

figures.name: Ibn Battuta
figures.role: Moroccan traveller who visited Mali in 1352 to 1353 and left a detailed description of the empire in the generation after Musa

images.caption: Mansa Musa seated with sceptre and golden nugget in the Catalan Atlas of Abraham Cresques, 1375. Bibliotheque nationale de France.
The Sankore Mosque in Timbuktu, a centre of Islamic learning developed in the generations after Mansa Musa's pilgrimage.
images.credit: Photograph by Ralf Steinberger, via Wikimedia Commons
